reason: Redis is mostly single-threaded, so a slow command blocks all other clients until it completes. The document recommends using Slow Log to detect blocking commands when diagnosing latency.
recommend: Inspect Slow Log and reduce or isolate slow commands, especially operations over large collections.

source.title: I've little time, give me the checklist
source.quote: Make sure you are not running slow commands that are blocking the server. Use the Redis Slow Log feature to check this.

reason: Transparent Huge Pages can introduce latency spikes in Redis workloads. The documentation explicitly says they must be disabled and Redis restarted afterward.
recommend: Disable Transparent Huge Pages at the kernel level and restart Redis.

source.title: I've little time, give me the checklist
source.quote: Transparent huge pages must be disabled from your kernel. Use echo never > /sys/kernel/mm/transparent_hugepage/enabled to disable them, and restart your Redis process.

reason: Forcing fsync on every write increases disk pressure and is explicitly described as very slow, which can cause latency spikes. A less aggressive fsync policy is recommended for better latency/performance tradeoffs.
recommend: Change AOF fsync policy from always to everysec, optionally combining it with no-appendfsync-on-rewrite yes to reduce rewrite-time disk pressure.

source.title: I've little time, give me the checklist
source.quote: AOF + fsync always: this is very slow, you should use it only if you know what you are doing. AOF + fsync every second: this is a good compromise.

reason: KEYS is a very common source of latency because Redis serves requests sequentially and expensive commands block other clients. The document says KEYS should only be used for debugging in production environments.
recommend: Replace KEYS with incremental iteration commands such as SCAN, SSCAN, HSCAN, or ZSCAN.

source.title: Latency generated by slow commands
source.quote: IMPORTANT NOTE: a VERY common source of latency generated by the execution of slow commands is the use of the KEYS command in production environments. KEYS, as documented in the Redis documentation, should only be used for debugging purposes.

reason: Without a memory limit, Redis can continue allocating memory until the host runs out of free RAM, risking system-wide memory starvation.
recommend: Set a maxmemory limit appropriate for the instance so Redis cannot consume all available memory.

source.title: Memory allocation
source.quote: If maxmemory is not set Redis will keep allocating memory as it sees fit and thus it can (gradually) eat up all your free memory. Therefore it is generally advisable to configure some limits.

reason: When Redis reaches its memory limit, using noeviction prevents the server from exhausting host memory and instead returns write errors that applications can handle.
recommend: Set maxmemory-policy to noeviction if you want Redis to reject writes at the limit rather than risk broader memory pressure effects.

source.title: Memory allocation
source.quote: You may also want to set maxmemory-policy to noeviction... It makes Redis return an out-of-memory error for write commands if and when it reaches the limit - which in turn may result in errors in the application but will not render the whole machine dead because of memory starvation.

reason: Small hashes use compact encodings and can consume far less memory than storing each attribute as a separate top-level key.
recommend: Model related small fields as hashes instead of many separate keys when field TTLs are not required.
fix: redis-cli HSET user:123 name Alice email alice@example.com password secret

source.title: Use hashes when possible
source.quote: Small hashes are encoded in a very small space, so you should try representing your data using hashes whenever possible.

metric_expr: CONFIG GET hash-max-listpack-entries OR CONFIG GET hash-max-ziplist-entries
threshold: significantly below workload small-hash field count
reason: Redis keeps small aggregates in memory-efficient special encodings only up to configured size limits; setting limits too low can force earlier conversion to normal encoding and lose memory savings.
recommend: Tune hash/listpack (or older ziplist) thresholds to fit your small-hash workload, and benchmark if increasing them for larger aggregates.

source.title: Special encoding of small aggregate data types
source.quote: If a specially encoded value overflows the configured max size, Redis will automatically convert it into normal encoding.

threshold: > 1.5 after large key deletions or workload shrink
reason: A high RSS-to-used-memory ratio after freeing data may reflect allocator behavior and past peak usage rather than an active Redis memory leak or immediately reclaimable memory.
recommend: Provision memory for peak usage and interpret fragmentation ratio cautiously after large deletions; do not rely on RSS dropping immediately when keys are removed.

source.title: Memory allocation
source.quote: Because of all this, the fragmentation ratio is not reliable when you had a memory usage that at the peak is much larger than the currently used memory.

reason: Disabling both AOF and RDB removes durability entirely, so Redis can lose all recent data on restart or crash. This is only appropriate for pure cache use cases.
recommend: Enable at least one persistence method; prefer both RDB and AOF when data safety matters.

source.title: Redis persistence
source.quote: No persistence: You can disable persistence completely. This is sometimes used when caching.

reason: With AOF enabled but fsync disabled, durability depends on the OS flush behavior and can lose substantially more data during a crash. The documentation recommends fsync every second as the default balance of safety and speed.
recommend: Set AOF fsync policy to everysec unless you explicitly accept lower durability.

source.title: How durable is the append only file?
source.quote: The suggested (and default) policy is to fsync every second. It is both fast and relatively safe.

reason: RDB-only persistence favors performance and restart speed, but it can lose the latest minutes of writes if Redis stops unexpectedly. This is a poor fit when you need strong durability.
recommend: Enable AOF in addition to RDB if you need better durability with limited data loss.

source.title: RDB disadvantages
source.quote: RDB is NOT good if you need to minimize the chance of data loss in case Redis stops working

metric_expr: persistence.aof_enabled == 1 AND persistence.aof_current_size / max(persistence.aof_base_size,1) > 2 AND persistence.aof_rewrite_in_progress == 0
reason: AOF grows with every write, and rewrite compacts it to the minimal command set needed to rebuild the dataset. If growth significantly exceeds the base size and no rewrite is running, restart and recovery overhead can increase.
recommend: Trigger an AOF rewrite or ensure automatic AOF rewrite is configured.

source.title: Log rewriting
source.quote: The AOF gets bigger and bigger as write operations are performed.

reason: Redis relies on successful fork() for background saving and replication-related child processes. If kernel overcommit is not enabled, fork can fail under memory pressure and background saving may break.
recommend: Enable Linux memory overcommit so Redis background save operations can fork reliably.

source.title: Redis setup tips Linux
source.quote: Set the Linux kernel overcommit memory setting to 1. Add vm.overcommit_memory = 1 to /etc/sysctl.conf.

reason: In write-heavy workloads, Redis may temporarily use up to twice its normal memory during RDB saves or AOF rewrites. If maxmemory is set too close to actual available RAM, background persistence can push the instance into memory pressure.
recommend: Lower maxmemory or add RAM so there is sufficient headroom for persistence overhead, especially on write-heavy instances.

source.title: Memory
source.quote: If you are using Redis in a write-heavy application, while saving an RDB file on disk or rewriting the AOF log, Redis can use up to 2 times the memory normally used.

reason: A replication backlog that is too small relative to dataset churn makes partial resynchronization less likely, forcing more expensive full syncs. A larger backlog helps replicas catch up more easily.
recommend: Increase replication backlog size in proportion to the amount of memory and write activity of the Redis primary.

source.title: Replication
source.quote: Set up a non-trivial replication backlog in proportion to the amount of memory Redis is using. The backlog allows replicas to sync with the primary (master) instance much more easily.

metric_expr: INFO persistence:rdb_last_bgsave_status, INFO persistence:aof_enabled, CONFIG GET save
reason: A master without persistence can restart with an empty dataset, and replicas may then replicate that empty state, causing data loss across the topology.
recommend: Enable persistence on the master, and preferably on replicas as well, when replication is used and data safety matters.

source.title: Safety of replication when master has persistence turned off
source.quote: In setups where Redis replication is used, it is strongly advised to have persistence turned on in the master and in the replicas.

metric_expr: INFO replication:role + INFO persistence:aof_enabled + CONFIG GET save + process supervisor autorestart setting
threshold: role = master AND persistence disabled AND process auto-restart enabled
reason: If a non-persistent master auto-restarts after a crash, it can come back empty before failover is detected, and replicas will sync the empty dataset, wiping valid data.
recommend: Disable automatic restart for Redis instances whenever replication is used with a master configured without persistence.
fix: systemctl disable redis && systemctl mask redis

source.title: Safety of replication when master has persistence turned off
source.quote: Every time data safety is important, and replication is used with master configured without persistence, auto restart of instances should be disabled.

metric_expr: INFO replication:master_sync_in_progress + OS disk latency/throughput metrics during full resync
threshold: full resynchronizations occur and disk latency is high / disks are slow
reason: Full resynchronization normally writes and reloads an RDB on disk, which can heavily stress the master when disks are slow.
recommend: Enable diskless replication so the child process streams the RDB directly to replicas instead of using disk as intermediate storage.

source.title: Diskless replication
source.quote: With slow disks this can be a very stressing operation for the master.

metric_expr: INFO commandstats + slowlog length on master for read-only O(N) commands
threshold: slow or heavy read-only O(N) operations observed on master
reason: Read-only replicas can absorb expensive read workloads, reducing load on the master and improving scalability.
recommend: Route slow read-only queries and O(N) operations to replicas instead of the master.

source.title: Important facts about Redis replication
source.quote: Replication can be used both for scalability, to have multiple replicas for read-only queries (for example, slow O(N) operations can be offloaded to replicas)

reason: When partial resynchronization is not possible, Redis must perform a full resynchronization, which requires creating and transferring a full dataset snapshot and is more expensive than incremental catch-up.
recommend: Investigate replication link instability and tune replication backlog/connection stability to favor partial resynchronization over full resynchronization.

source.title: Redis replication
source.quote: When a partial resynchronization is not possible, the replica will ask for a full resynchronization. This will involve a more complex process in which the master needs to create a snapshot of all its data, send it to the replica, and then continue sending the stream of commands as the dataset changes.

reason: Replication and AOF buffers are excluded from the memory compared to maxmemory, so setting maxmemory too close to total RAM can still cause memory pressure outside the eviction accounting.
recommend: Lower maxmemory to leave free RAM for replication/AOF buffers, using mem_not_counted_for_evict as the sizing input.
fix: redis-cli INFO memory && redis-cli CONFIG SET maxmemory <total_ram_minus_buffer_headroom>

source.title: Setting maxmemory for a replicated or persisted instance
source.quote: If you are using replication or persistence, we recommend that you set maxmemory to leave a little RAM free to store the buffers.

reason: A lower-than-expected cache hit rate indicates the cache is not retaining the right data long enough, often due to a poor eviction policy choice.
recommend: Review and change maxmemory-policy to better match access patterns; allkeys-lru is the recommended default when a hot subset dominates accesses.

source.title: Using the INFO command
source.quote: If the percentage of hits is lower than expected, then this might mean you are not using the best eviction policy.

threshold: policy IN (volatile-lru, volatile-lfu, volatile-random, volatile-ttl) AND db0.expires == 0
reason: Volatile eviction policies only consider keys with TTLs; if no keys have expiration, the policy effectively behaves like noeviction and new writes can fail under memory pressure.
recommend: Either assign expirations to cache keys or switch to an allkeys policy that can evict any key.

source.title: Eviction policies
source.quote: The volatile-xxx policies behave like noeviction if no keys have an associated expiration.

metric_expr: CONFIG GET maxmemory-policy -> policy; CONFIG GET maxmemory-samples -> samples; INFO stats: keyspace_misses
threshold: policy IN (allkeys-lru, volatile-lru) AND samples < 10 AND keyspace_misses is elevated
reason: A small LRU sample size can make eviction less accurate; increasing samples can more closely approximate true LRU at some CPU cost and may reduce misses.
recommend: Test a higher maxmemory-samples value, such as 10, for LRU-based policies and observe whether cache misses improve.

source.title: Approximated LRU algorithm
source.quote: However you can raise the sample size to 10 at the cost of some additional CPU usage to closely approximate true LRU, and check if this makes a difference in your cache misses rate.

reason: Running Redis with only built-in defaults is documented as suitable for testing and development, not proper production configuration. This increases the risk of non-persistent or unintended settings after restart.
recommend: Start Redis with a redis.conf or redis-full.conf file and persist intended settings there.

source.title: Redis configuration Overview of redis.conf, the Redis configuration file
source.quote: Redis is able to start without a configuration file using a built-in default configuration, however this setup is only recommended for testing and development purposes. The proper way to configure Redis is by providing a Redis configuration file, usually called redis.conf.

reason: Changing configuration with CONFIG SET only affects the running server and will be lost on restart unless the config files are updated. This can cause performance-related settings to revert unexpectedly after failover or maintenance.
recommend: After changing supported settings at runtime, persist them to disk by updating redis.conf/redis-full.conf or using CONFIG REWRITE.

source.title: Changing Redis configuration while the server is running
source.quote: Note that modifying the configuration on the fly does not affect the redis.conf and redis-full.conf files, so at the next restart of Redis, the old configuration will be used instead. Make sure to also modify the configuration files accordingly to the configuration you set using CONFIG SET.

reason: For cache workloads, the document recommends setting a memory limit so Redis can evict keys when the limit is reached. Without maxmemory, memory usage can grow without a configured eviction boundary.
recommend: Set maxmemory to an explicit limit appropriate for the host or container memory budget.
fix: redis-cli CONFIG SET maxmemory 2mb && redis-cli CONFIG REWRITE

source.title: Configuring Redis as a cache
source.quote: If you plan to use Redis as a cache where every key will have an expire set, you may consider using the following configuration instead (assuming a max memory limit of 2 megabytes as an example): maxmemory 2mb

reason: For cache use, the document explicitly recommends allkeys-lru so keys are evicted automatically under the memory limit. Using a different policy may not match the intended cache behavior described by the documentation.
recommend: For pure cache workloads, set the eviction policy to allkeys-lru.
fix: redis-cli CONFIG SET maxmemory-policy allkeys-lru && redis-cli CONFIG REWRITE

source.title: Configuring Redis as a cache
source.quote: If you plan to use Redis as a cache where every key will have an expire set, you may consider using the following configuration instead (assuming a max memory limit of 2 megabytes as an example): maxmemory 2mb maxmemory-policy allkeys-lru

metric_expr: (config.bind in ['0.0.0.0','*'] OR config.bind contains non-loopback interface) AND connected_clients > 0
reason: Exposing Redis directly to untrusted networks can allow destructive commands such as FLUSHALL and unauthorized access to data. The documentation says Redis should only be reachable by trusted clients and firewalled from the outside.
recommend: Restrict network exposure by binding Redis to loopback or trusted interfaces only, and firewall the Redis port from external access.

source.title: Network security
source.quote: the Redis port should be firewalled to prevent access from the outside. Clients will still be able to access Redis using the loopback interface. Note that it is possible to bind Redis to a single interface by adding a line like the following to the redis.conf file: bind 127.0.0.1

reason: With default all-interface binding and no password, disabling protected mode removes Redis's safeguard that limits access to loopback clients. This increases the risk of accidental internet exposure and unauthorized access.
recommend: Enable protected mode unless the instance is explicitly secured by binding, firewalling, and authentication.

source.title: Protected mode
source.quote: Since version 3.2.0, Redis enters a special mode called protected mode when it is executed with the default configuration (binding all the interfaces) and without any password in order to access it. In this mode, Redis only replies to queries from the loopback interfaces

reason: Authentication provides a redundant protection layer if firewalling or other network controls fail. Without ACLs or a password, unauthenticated clients can issue commands if they reach the instance.
recommend: Configure Redis authentication, preferably ACLs in Redis 6+, or at minimum set a strong requirepass password.

source.title: Authentication
source.quote: The recommended authentication method, introduced in Redis 6, is via Access Control Lists, allowing named users to be created and assigned fine-grained permissions... The goal of the authentication layer is to optionally provide a layer of redundancy. If firewalling or any other system implemented to protect Redis from external attackers fails, an external client will still not be able to access the Redis instance without knowledge of the authentication password.

reason: AUTH and other Redis commands are sent unencrypted unless TLS is enabled, so credentials and data can be exposed to network eavesdropping. TLS protects client, replication, and cluster communications.
recommend: Enable TLS for Redis connections, especially when traffic may traverse untrusted networks.

source.title: Authentication
source.quote: Since the AUTH command, like every other Redis command, is sent unencrypted, it does not protect against an attacker who has enough access to the network to perform eavesdropping.

reason: Allowing clients to use CONFIG can let them alter server behavior, including paths used for persistence, which the documentation identifies as a security issue. Restricting dangerous commands reduces the blast radius of compromised or overprivileged clients.
recommend: Use ACL rules to disallow dangerous administrative commands such as CONFIG; if ACLs are unavailable, rename or shadow the command.

source.title: Disallowing specific commands
source.quote: Warning: The method is deprecated and may be removed in future versions. Instead, use ACL rules to disallow specific commands.

reason: With latency monitoring disabled, Redis will not log latency spikes, making it harder to detect and troubleshoot blocking events affecting response time.
recommend: Enable latency monitoring with a threshold aligned to your application's maximum acceptable latency.

source.title: How to enable latency monitoring
source.quote: Monitoring is turned off by default (threshold set to 0)

reason: If the configured threshold is higher than the application's acceptable latency, Redis will miss events that already violate service expectations.
recommend: Set latency-monitor-threshold to the maximum acceptable latency in milliseconds for the application.

source.title: How to enable latency monitoring
source.quote: if the application requires a maximum acceptable latency of 100 milliseconds, the threshold should be set to log all the events blocking the server for a time equal or greater to 100 milliseconds.

reason: Latency spikes on regular commands indicate slow command executions that can block the single Redis thread and increase client response times.
recommend: Identify the offending commands via latency reports and reduce use of slow commands or rewrite access patterns to avoid blocking operations.

source.title: Events and time series
source.quote: command is an event that measures latency spikes of possibly slow command executions

reason: Latency spikes in O(1) and O(log N) commands suggest unexpected blocking or system-level interference, since these commands are expected to be fast.
recommend: Inspect latency analysis and investigate contention or background work affecting normally fast commands.

source.title: Events and time series
source.quote: fast-command is the event name for the monitoring of the O(1) and O(log N) commands.

reason: High fork latency indicates the fork(2) system call is taking too long, which can contribute to Redis latency spikes during persistence-related operations.
recommend: Investigate OS and memory conditions affecting fork performance and review persistence behavior during spikes.

source.title: Events and time series
source.quote: the fork event only monitors the time taken by Redis to execute the fork(2) system call.
